stem: In the additive group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) of integers modulo [[n]], what is the order of the element [[k]] (the least positive integer \( d \) with \( d \cdot [[k]] \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \))?
solution: The order of \( k \) in \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is \( \frac{n}{\gcd(n,k)} \). Here \( \frac{[[n]]}{\gcd([[n]],[[k]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \gcd([[n]],[[k]]) \), not the order; the order is \( n/\gcd(n,k) \).
This assumes every element has order equal to the group size, which only holds for generators.
This confuses the element with its order.
This subtracts the element from the modulus for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: Consider the element \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \) in the direct product group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). The order of \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \) is the least common multiple of the order of [[a]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) and the order of [[b]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). What is the order of \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \)?
solution: The order of [[a]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) is \( [[m]]/\gcd([[m]],[[a]]) \) and the order of [[b]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is \( [[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[b]]) \). The order of the pair is the lcm of these two orders, which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two component orders instead of taking their lcm.
This uses only the first component's order, ignoring the second.
This uses only the second component's order, ignoring the first.
This multiplies the group sizes, not the component orders' lcm.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: Let \( x \) and \( y \) be COMMUTING elements of a group, with \( x \) of order [[a]] and \( y \) of order [[b]], where \( \gcd([[a]],[[b]]) = 1 \). What is the order of the product \( xy \)?
solution: For commuting elements of coprime orders, \( \operatorname{ord}(xy) = \operatorname{ord}(x)\cdot\operatorname{ord}(y) \) (since it equals \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]],[[b]]) \), which is just the product when \( \gcd([[a]],[[b]])=1 \)). So the order of \( xy \) is \( [[a]] \cdot [[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two orders instead of multiplying them.
This uses only \( \operatorname{ord}(x) \), ignoring \( y \).
This uses only \( \operatorname{ord}(y) \), ignoring \( x \).
Since the orders are coprime, their gcd is 1, not the order of the product.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: An element \( x \) of a group has order [[n]]. What is the SMALLEST positive integer \( m \) such that \( x^{m} = x^{-1} \)?
solution: \( x^{m} = x^{-1} \) iff \( x^{m+1} = e \) iff \( [[n]] \mid (m+1) \); the smallest positive such \( m \) is \( m = [[n]] - 1 \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( x^{n} = e \ne x^{-1} \) (unless \( n=1 \)), confusing the order with the exponent needed here.
This overshoots past a full period.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.
This mistakenly halves the order, which only works for elements of order 2.
This confuses \( x^{-1} \) with \( x^{1} \).
This just restates \( n \), not \( n - 1 \).
